text_blocks.text: In 1929, the university's fifth president, Robert
Maynard Hutchins, took office; the university underwent many
changes during his 24-year tenure. Hutchins eliminated varsity
football from the university in an attempt to emphasize
academics over athletics, instituted the undergraduate college's
liberal-arts curriculum known as the Common Core, and
organized the university's graduate work into its current[when?]
four divisions. In 1933, Hutchins proposed an unsuccessful plan
to merge the University of Chicago and Northwestern
University into a single university. During his term, the
University of Chicago Hospitals (now called the University of
Chicago Medical Center) finished construction and enrolled its
first medical students. Also, the Committee on Social Thought,
an institution distinctive of the university, was created.
